critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experiment design with meaningful attention to temporal leakage, label delay, and evaluation rigor. I'll focus on genuine methodological concerns rather than nits.

**Issue 1: The comparison is not feature-controlled — it is actually a joint test of (architecture) × (feature set)**

The methodology frames this as a sequence model vs. gradient-boosted baseline comparison, but the two models differ in two simultaneous dimensions: (a) model architecture and (b) available features. The sequence model receives the full 90-day transaction history as input; the baseline does not. The design documents this partially in the 'Feature parity audit' section and calls it 'a feature enhancement, not a model advantage,' but this framing does not resolve the confound — it names it.

The claim being made is that any observed PR-AUC gap is attributable to the sequence model's architectural capacity. The mechanism of failure is that the true driver of lift could be purely the historical sequence features, not the architecture. A gradient-boosted model fed handcrafted historical features (e.g., last-N transaction amounts, time-since-last-transaction, merchant-frequency ratios over 90 days) might close most or all of the gap. Without this ablation baseline — GBT + sequence-derived features vs. sequence model + same features — the experiment cannot attribute lift to the model class rather than to the information advantage.

Evidence one way or the other: Run a third arm — a GBT model augmented with summary statistics derived from the same 90-day window (mean/max/std of amount, count by category, time-gap statistics, recency-weighted sums). If the gap largely closes, the architectural hypothesis is unsupported. If it persists, the architecture is doing real work the tabular summary cannot replicate.

**Issue 2: Velocity features have a subtle look-ahead risk under the described construction**

The design states: 'Velocity features: transaction count and sum in the last 7 and 30 days (computed from training-period windows only; no future aggregation).' The phrase 'computed from training-period windows only' is ambiguous. If it means that for a transaction occurring in month 3 of training, its velocity window only looks back within the training period, this is correct. But if it means velocity features are computed in a single batch pass over the training set, there is a risk that the last few days of the training window use data that bleeds slightly into the validation boundary — this is a minor edge case but present.

More importantly, for a transaction at time T in the training set, the 7-day and 30-day velocity counts should be computed from transactions strictly before T. If the velocity computation is done via a rolling window applied to the full sorted dataset, this is correct. If it is done by joining on a pre-aggregated table computed over a time range (a common implementation shortcut), there is a risk of within-window leakage depending on how the aggregate is defined. The design does not specify the implementation mechanism.

Evidence: Verify that for each transaction in the training set, the velocity aggregation uses only transactions with timestamps strictly less than the current transaction's timestamp. The correct check is: for a known fraudster who commits multiple frauds in a burst, does the velocity feature for their first fraud in the burst reflect 0 prior transactions that day? If it reflects >0, leakage is present.

**Issue 3: The bidirectional LSTM/Transformer architecture is inappropriate for deployment and creates an evaluation artifact**

The design specifies a bidirectional LSTM or Transformer as the sequence encoder. Bidirectionality requires access to future transactions in the sequence relative to the current transaction being scored. In a real-time fraud scoring context, the 'sequence' is the 90 days of prior transactions, all of which are already historical — so bidirectionality across that historical sequence is actually fine and not look-ahead in a data-leakage sense.

However, this does introduce a subtle inconsistency: the Transformer with full self-attention across the 90-day sequence is treating all past transactions as equally accessible. In production, the sequence presented at scoring time is exactly the historical window, so there is no data leakage in the strict sense. This issue is moot if deployment uses the same frozen historical window.

I retract this as a leakage concern — it is not one. It remains a deployment complexity concern (latency, sequence construction at inference time) but that is out of scope.

**Issue 4: Merchant embedding vocabulary is fit on training data only, but merchant ID frequency distribution is likely extremely long-tailed, creating a severe OOV problem on the test set**

The design correctly fits embedding vocabularies on the training set only. However, for merchant IDs specifically, the long-tail distribution in retail card transactions means that a meaningful fraction of test-set transactions will involve merchants not seen during training. The design does not specify how out-of-vocabulary (OOV) merchants are handled in the sequence model's embeddings.

This matters asymmetrically: the GBT baseline with one-hot encoding will also suffer from OOV merchants but has a well-defined behavior (the merchant column is 0 for all known categories, treated as 'unknown'). The sequence model's learned merchant embeddings may degrade more severely for OOV merchants if the OOV token embedding is poorly initialized or undertrained. More importantly, if new merchants appearing in the test period have a different fraud rate than training-period merchants (which is plausible — new merchants are often targets of fraud rings exploiting the novelty), the sequence model may be systematically disadvantaged on exactly the cases that matter most operationally.

Evidence: Report OOV merchant rate in validation and test sets. Report stratified PR-AUC for known vs. OOV merchants separately for both models. If the sequence model underperforms GBT specifically on OOV merchants, this is a deployment-relevant failure mode the aggregate PR-AUC will obscure.

**Issue 5: The 3-month test window may not provide adequate power for a meaningful PR-AUC bootstrap confidence interval given the low base rate**

At 0.1–0.5% fraud prevalence over 3 months of retail transaction volume, the absolute number of positive (fraud) examples in the test set depends heavily on total transaction volume. If the processor handles, say, 10M transactions per month, 3 months yields ~30M transactions and 30K–150K fraud cases — adequate for stable PR-AUC estimation. But for smaller processors, 3 months at 0.1% prevalence might yield only a few hundred fraud cases, at which point bootstrap confidence intervals on PR-AUC become very wide and the comparison becomes underpowered.

The design does not report the expected number of positive test examples, which is necessary to assess whether the comparison will be statistically meaningful. PR-AUC is particularly sensitive to sample size at low positive counts because the precision-recall curve is estimated from a small number of positive examples.

Evidence: Report the expected positive count in the test set. If it is below ~1,000 positive examples, the experiment should either use a longer test window or acknowledge that the power to detect modest improvements (e.g., 0.01–0.02 PR-AUC absolute) is low.

**Summary:**

The most fundamental flaw is Issue 1: the experiment as designed cannot distinguish between 'sequence architectures are better' and 'having 90 days of raw transaction history as input is better.' This is not a minor confound — it is the central question, and the missing GBT+feature-engineered-history baseline is necessary to answer it. Issues 4 and 5 are genuine operational and statistical concerns but are secondary. Issue 2 is a real implementation risk that the design leaves underspecified.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Anticipated Criticisms

### 1. Bidirectional LSTM/Transformer for real-time fraud detection — temporal leakage in the sequence encoder?

**Claim a critic would make:** A bidirectional sequence encoder (BiLSTM or bidirectional Transformer) looks at future transactions within the 90-day window when encoding any given transaction in the sequence. This means the model sees transactions that happened *after* the transaction being scored, which is impossible at inference time and constitutes feature leakage.

**Validity:** This is a REAL and SERIOUS flaw. The methodology proposes a bidirectional encoder to process the 90-day history leading up to the current transaction. If the current transaction is the target transaction, and the sequence is the 90-day history *prior* to it, then bidirectionality within the history window is not leakage — we are looking backward in time, not forward relative to the target. However, if the sequence includes the target transaction itself or transactions after it, there is leakage. The prompt says 'the last 90 days of transactions' and the current transaction's tabular features are concatenated separately, which suggests the sequence is the history *excluding* the current transaction. In that case, bidirectionality within the history window is fine — you can look at the full history sequence in both directions because all those transactions are prior to the target.

**Assessment:** The design intent appears sound — encode the prior 90 days, concatenate with the current transaction's features. Bidirectionality within a purely historical window does not leak future information relative to the scoring moment. However, this assumption must be explicitly stated and verified in implementation. The methodology does not clearly spell this out, which is a presentation risk, not necessarily a design flaw. I'll flag it as needing clarification but not as a critical flaw.

**Remaining uncertainty:** The document is ambiguous about whether the current transaction is included in the sequence or not. This needs explicit documentation.

---

### 2. Feature asymmetry between sequence model and baseline — unfair comparison

**Claim a critic would make:** The sequence model has access to 90 days of full transaction history (raw sequences, learned embeddings), while the baseline only has hand-crafted velocity features (7-day and 30-day counts/sums). This is not a fair comparison between model architectures — it's a comparison between richer features + sequence model vs. simpler features + GBT.

**Validity:** This is a legitimate and important concern. The methodology partially acknowledges it under 'Feature parity audit' but does not fully resolve it. The question being answered is: 'Does a sequence model with learned history representations outperform a GBT with manual velocity features?' That is a valid research question, but it conflates architectural advantage with feature advantage.

A stronger design would include a third condition: GBT with richer hand-crafted features derived from the full 90-day history (e.g., mean/std/max transaction amount, merchant diversity, temporal gap statistics, top-N category counts). If the sequence model beats this enriched GBT baseline, the architectural advantage is more credibly demonstrated.

**Assessment:** This is a real flaw in the comparison, but it may be a *scoped* one. If the paper's stated objective is 'does the sequence model + learned embeddings outperform the tabular GBT baseline used in production,' then comparing against the production baseline is the right question. But the methodology doesn't clearly state whether the GBT is meant to represent the current production system or the best possible tabular alternative. This conflation is worth acknowledging but does not invalidate the experiment if the objective is narrowly framed.

**Remaining uncertainty:** The degree to which 7/30-day velocity features vs. full 90-day history features drives the gap is unresolvable without an ablation.

---

### 3. Hyperparameter tuning asymmetry — same effort does not mean same compute budget

**Claim a critic would make:** 'Same hyperparameter tuning effort' is ambiguous. A GBT grid search over 50 configurations takes minutes; a Transformer grid search over 50 configurations takes days. Equal effort by number of trials is not equal effort by wall-clock time or compute investment, and this systematically disadvantages the GBT baseline.

**Validity:** Partially valid, but the direction of bias is actually reversed from what the critic implies. GBT hyperparameter tuning is computationally cheap, meaning the practitioner can explore far more configurations in the same clock time. The sequence model tuning is expensive, meaning it is more likely to be under-tuned. The methodology says 'same effort' without specifying what 'effort' means — if it means same number of trials, GBT is likely more thoroughly tuned. If it means same wall-clock time, GBT wins by an even wider margin.

This is a known limitation of any deep learning vs. classical ML comparison. The typical mitigation is to run a fixed number of random/Bayesian trials and report the number of trials explicitly. The methodology does not specify this. It's a reporting flaw, not a fatal design flaw — the comparison can still be valid if documented.

**Assessment:** Acknowledged flaw in reporting, not fatal.

---

### 4. Single train/val/test split — no robustness estimate across time windows

**Claim a critic would make:** The methodology uses a single 3-month test window. Fraud patterns are highly non-stationary (e.g., seasonal spikes, new fraud vectors). A single test window may be lucky or unlucky for either model. Walk-forward cross-validation across multiple test windows would give a more robust estimate of relative performance.

**Validity:** This is a valid concern. A single 3-month holdout is sensitive to temporal distribution shift that may specifically favor or disfavor either architecture. The methodology does report bootstrap confidence intervals, which quantifies uncertainty around point estimates within the window, but does not address uncertainty across different time windows.

However, multi-window evaluation is computationally expensive for sequence models and is frequently omitted in industry ML papers. The design is honest about using a single split. Bootstrap CIs are a partial mitigation. This is a known and scoped limitation.

**Assessment:** Valid limitation, not a fatal flaw for the stated objective. Should be documented in limitations section.

---

### 5. Label delay and incomplete labeling — survivorship bias in the training set

**Claim a critic would make:** Fraud labels take 3–14 days to arrive. By excluding the last 60 days of data before cutoff, the methodology creates a clean labeling window. But this means the model is trained on a dataset where late-arriving fraud in the tail of the training window is missing (not yet labeled at training time), creating a distribution mismatch between training and deployment conditions where recent fraud labels are always incomplete.

**Validity:** The 60-day exclusion buffer is actually a *mitigation* for label delay, not a source of it. By dropping the 60 days before cutoff, the methodology ensures that all included transactions have had at least 46+ days (60 - 14 = 46) to receive their labels, which covers the full labeling window. This is a well-designed choice.

However, the critic is right that in *deployment*, the model will score transactions with zero label delay accommodation — it must decide at authorization time. The training data, with its clean 60-day buffer, represents a more complete labeling world than deployment will see. This is the inherent tension of delayed-label fraud detection and is not solvable by the experiment design — it is a property of the operational setting. The methodology acknowledges this implicitly.

**Assessment:** Design choice is sound. The fundamental tension is inherent to the problem, not a flaw in the experiment.

---

### 6. Embedding leakage — embeddings fit on full corpus?

**Claim a critic would make:** If merchant and category embeddings are learned end-to-end, and the model is trained on months 1–12 only (with embeddings fit on training data only, as specified), this should be fine. But the document says 'vocabulary fit on training data only' — this is about the vocabulary (which merchants exist), not the embedding weights. The embedding weights are learned during sequence model training, which is done on training data only. So this appears fine.

**Validity:** The methodology is careful here — it says 'fit on training set only' for all transformers and that embedding vocabularies are fit on training data. End-to-end learned embeddings are fit during model training on the training set, so no leakage. The 'Embedding leakage check' section even explicitly calls this out. The design is sound.

**Assessment:** No flaw here — the methodology anticipates and addresses this.

---

### 7. Imbalance handling — no mention of oversampling, class weighting, or threshold optimization

**Claim a critic would make:** With 0.1–0.5% fraud prevalence, models trained with default loss functions will be heavily biased toward predicting non-fraud. The methodology doesn't specify class weighting, SMOTE, or any imbalance handling.

**Validity:** The methodology uses PR-AUC as its primary metric, which is robust to class imbalance and doesn't require threshold selection. The threshold is chosen on the validation set (F1-optimized threshold is a secondary metric). For GBT models (XGBoost/LightGBM), scale_pos_weight or class_weight parameters are standard and likely used in practice but not mentioned. For the sequence model, class-weighted loss is standard.

This is a real presentation gap: the methodology should specify imbalance handling. However, since PR-AUC is threshold-agnostic and the evaluation protocol is otherwise sound, the comparison is valid as long as both models are given the same imbalance handling or none — the relative comparison holds even if both are suboptimally tuned for imbalance (both are equally disadvantaged).

**Assessment:** Presentation gap, not fatal. Should be documented.

---

### 8. Statistical significance testing — bootstrap on a temporal dataset

**Claim a critic would make:** Bootstrap resampling on a temporally ordered test set assumes independence across samples. Fraud episodes often cluster by account or merchant, violating independence. Cluster-robust or block bootstrap would be more appropriate.

**Validity:** This is a real statistical concern. Standard bootstrap resamples transactions independently, but transactions from the same account or merchant are correlated. This means confidence intervals may be too narrow (underestimating uncertainty). Block bootstrap (resampling account-level or merchant-level clusters) would be more conservative and appropriate.

**Assessment:** This is a genuine statistical flaw in the significance testing protocol. It is not a fatal flaw for the point estimates of PR-AUC (those are unaffected), but it means the confidence intervals may be anti-conservative. This should be acknowledged and ideally corrected.

---

### 9. Min-max normalization for amounts — sensitivity to outliers

**Claim a critic would make:** Min-max scaling of transaction amounts is sensitive to extreme outliers in the training data. A single very large transaction amount will compress the entire distribution into a small range, degrading model performance for mid-range amounts.

**Validity:** This is a known weakness of min-max scaling vs. robust scaling (e.g., quantile normalization). However, both models receive the same preprocessing, so the relative comparison is unaffected. The absolute performance of both models may be suboptimal, but the comparison is still valid. For a production system, quantile normalization would be preferred.

**Assessment:** Suboptimal but symmetrical — does not threaten the validity of the comparison.

---

### 10. No ablation study — can't decompose gains

**Claim a critic would make:** If the sequence model wins, we don't know whether the gain comes from (a) the sequential modeling of transaction history, (b) the learned embeddings, (c) the richer feature set (90-day history vs. 7/30-day velocity), or (d) model capacity (300-500K parameters vs. GBT). Without ablations, the conclusion 'sequence models outperform GBT' is uninterpretable.

**Validity:** This is a strong and valid criticism. The methodology provides stratified breakdowns and secondary metrics but no ablation conditions. At minimum, a GBT with 90-day engineered features would decompose architectural advantage from feature advantage. An attention-only sequence model vs. LSTM would decompose architectural choices.

This is a significant gap if the objective is to understand *why* one approach works better. If the objective is purely operational ('should we deploy the sequence model?'), then the ablation is less critical — we just need to know which model performs better under operationally realistic conditions.

**Assessment:** Critical gap for scientific contribution; less critical for operational decision-making. Given the stated objective ('evaluate whether a sequence model outperforms a GBT baseline'), this is a scoped limitation but limits interpretability of findings.

---

## Summary of Critical Issues Found in Pass 1

1. **Bidirectionality ambiguity** — needs clarification but likely sound by design intent
2. **Feature asymmetry (real flaw)** — sequence model gets richer features; ablation needed to separate architectural from feature advantage
3. **Hyperparameter tuning operationalization** — 'same effort' is underspecified
4. **Single test window** — limited robustness to temporal non-stationarity (documented limitation)
5. **Imbalance handling** — not specified in methodology
6. **Bootstrap CI validity** — standard bootstrap ignores temporal/cluster correlation; CIs may be anti-conservative
7. **No ablation study** — cannot decompose the source of any observed gain

Issues 2, 6, and 7 are genuine design or analytical flaws. Issues 3, 4, and 5 are documentation gaps or known limitations. Issue 1 is a presentation risk.

The methodology is largely well-structured (temporal split, PR-AUC, test set sequestration, distribution shift analysis), but the feature asymmetry and lack of ablations mean that a win by the sequence model cannot be conclusively attributed to architectural superiority vs. feature richness. The bootstrap CI issue means statistical significance claims may be overconfident.

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

The methodology is sound in its evaluation infrastructure (temporal split, PR-AUC, test sequestration, calibration, stratified evaluation) and answers the narrow operational question. However, there are multiple genuine unaddressed flaws:

- Feature asymmetry (not resolvable by analysis alone — requires empirical ablation)
- Anti-conservative confidence intervals (requires corrected bootstrap)
- Undecomposable gains (requires ablation conditions)

These are not fatal for the operational question but significantly limit the scientific interpretation of results. The key question — 'does feature advantage vs. architecture advantage drive any observed gain?' — cannot be answered without empirical testing. The verdict is `empirical_test_agreed` because the core scientific claim requires additional experiments (ablation with enriched GBT baseline) to validate, even though the evaluation protocol itself is largely sound.
all_issues_raised: The comparison conflates architecture with feature set: the sequence model receives richer inputs than the GBT baseline, so observed lift cannot be attributed to architecture alone without a GBT+handcrafted-history ablation arm.

Velocity feature computation is underspecified: batch-computed rolling aggregates risk within-window leakage if implementation joins on pre-aggregated tables rather than strictly-before-T transaction counts.

Merchant embedding OOV handling is unspecified: new merchants in the test period — who may carry elevated fraud risk — will map to an undertrained OOV token, creating an asymmetric failure mode not reflected in aggregate PR-AUC.

The absolute positive count in the 3-month test set is unreported, leaving open whether the bootstrap confidence interval on PR-AUC will have adequate power to detect meaningful improvements at low fraud prevalence.
all_issues_adjudicated: The comparison conflates architecture with feature set: the sequence model receives the full 90-day transaction history while the GBT baseline receives only hand-crafted 7/30-day velocity features, so any observed PR-AUC gap cannot be attributed to architectural advantage without a GBT+handcrafted-90-day-history ablation arm — the defender concedes this is a real flaw and that it requires empirical testing to resolve.

Velocity feature computation is underspecified: the design does not clarify whether aggregates are computed strictly before each transaction's timestamp or via a batch join that could introduce within-window leakage, and the defender does not address this specific issue.

Merchant embedding OOV handling is unspecified: new merchants in the test period may carry elevated fraud risk yet map to an undertrained OOV token, creating an asymmetric failure mode not reflected in aggregate PR-AUC — the defender does not address this issue.

The absolute positive count in the 3-month test set is unreported, leaving open whether the bootstrap confidence interval on PR-AUC will have adequate statistical power to detect meaningful improvements at low fraud prevalence — the defender does not address this issue.
